figures.caption: Fig. 3: Real-Time Perception. (a) Distributed human pose estimation: each of 12 edge nodes runs YOLO-based detection and RTMPose for 2D whole-body keypoints; a central server triangulates 3D joints via RANSAC multi-view reconstruction. (b) Object 6D pose estimation from a calibrated stereo pair. Meshes are precomputed via MV-SAM3D [5] (top); at runtime, FoundationStereo provides metric depth and FoundationPose performs marker-free 6D tracking at 6 Hz (bottom). (c) Live system operation with concurrent human activity and robotic actuation. (d) Human pose throughput and per-module latency vs. number of tracked humans (40 cameras). (e) Object 6D tracking throughput and per-module latency vs. number of tracked objects (1 stereo pair).

Fig. 5: Behavior Learning. (a) Safety vs. accumulated behavior memory: (i, ii) mark non-monotonic transitions. (b) Intent-aware placement accuracy vs. demonstration count: (iii, iv) mark failure modes at low counts. (c) Per-subject top-down occupancy at cumulative quartiles.
Table 3: Reprojection error: Ours vs. COLMAP.

Fig. 7: Effect of camera count. We subsample the 48-camera set via farthest-point removal. (a, b) Insufficiently observed joints decrease with camera count; nearly all are reconstructable above 40 cameras. (c) Worst-case per-joint visibility: extremities need the most cameras; above 45, all joints have ≥2 views. (d, e) Triangulation error bound (1/ sin θ) stabilizes beyond 35 cameras. (f) Average observing cameras per joint across subsets. (g) Voxel coverage vs. camera count: at 48 cameras, 80%+ voxels have ≥4 views. (h) Top-down visibility from 12 to 48 cameras; full density eliminates dead zones across the 23.1 m2 room.

Fig. 4: Task Overview. (a) Safety-aware coexistence: two arms sort food items in a shared kitchen while a human moves freely; each arm yields, reroutes, or pauses based on real-time 3D human state. (b) Human-anticipatory assistance: given partial demonstrations of a human sorting objects, the robot must infer the placement rule and predict the correct destination for each remaining item.

Fig. 6: Intention-Aware Transfer. From top to bottom: (a) Human notices a dry plant; the robot infers the need and delivers a watering kettle. (b) Human prepares to eat a hot dog; the robot retrieves and hands over a mustard bottle. (c) Human eating chips gestures for a drink; the robot identifies and delivers a soft drink.

Fig. 2: System overview of OmniRobotHome. 48 hardware-synchronized cameras across 12 edge nodes provide real-time markerless 3D perception of humans, objects, and robots in a unified world frame. Details in Sec. 3.
